questions.question_text: Mansi purchased a car for ₹ 2,50,000 and sold it for ₹ 3,48,000. What is the percent profit she made on the car?
If C.P. is ₹ 2516 and S.P. is ₹ 2272, find the percentage loss.

Find S.P., when
(i) C.P. = ₹ 56.25, Gain = 20%
(ii) C.P. = ₹ 80.40, Loss = 15%
A gold bracelet is sold for ₹ 14500 at a loss of 20%. What is the cost price of the gold bracelet?
The owner of a cell phone charges his customer 23% more than the cost price. If a customer paid ₹ 7011 for a cell phone, then what was the cost price of the cell phone?
Shaloo sold a mobile phone at the cost of ₹ 1950 at a loss of 25%. At what cost will she have to sell it to get a profit of 30%?
questions.solution:
Case I. S.P. = 1950, Loss = 25%.
C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{75}\times1950\right)$ = ₹ 2600.
Case II. C.P. = ₹ 2600, Profit = 30%.
S.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{130}{100}\times2600\right)$ = ₹ 3380.
Another Method:
Let the new S.P. be ₹ x. Then,
(100 – loss%) : (1st S.P.) = (100 + gain%) : (2nd S.P.)
⇒ (100 – 25) : 1950 = (100 + 30) : x
⇒ 75 : 1950 = 130 : x ⇒ x = $\left(\frac{1950\times130}{75}\right)$ = 3380.

questions.question_text: A television manufacturer earns 20% profit by selling each T.V. set for ₹ 14400. If the production cost is increased by 15%, what should be the new selling price of a set so as to gain 15%?
A book was sold for ₹ 27.50 with a profit of 10%. If it were sold for ₹ 25.75, then what would have been the percentage of profit or loss?
If the cost price is 96% of the selling price, then what is the profit percent?
questions.solution:
Let S.P. = ₹ 100. Then, C.P. = ₹ 96; Profit = ₹ 4.
Profit% = $\left(\frac{4}{96}\times100\right)$ % = $\frac{25}{6}$ % = 4.17%.

questions.question_text: A manufacturer makes 800 articles at a cost of ₹ 1.50 per article. He fixes the selling price such that if only 600 articles are sold, he would make a profit of 30% on his outlay. However, he sold 620 articles at this price. Find his actual profit percent of the total outlay, assuming that the unsold articles are useless.
questions.solution:
C.P. of 800 articles = ₹ (1.50 × 800) = ₹ 1200.
S.P. of 600 articles = ₹ $\left(\frac{130}{100}\times1200\right)$ = ₹ 1560.
S.P. of 620 articles = ₹ $\left(\frac{1560}{600}\times620\right)$ = ₹ 1612.
Profit = ₹ (1612 – 1200) = ₹ 412.
Profit % = $\left(\frac{412}{1200}\times100\right)$ % = 34 $\frac{1}{3}$ %.

questions.question_text: The selling price of 30 items is equal to the purchase price of 25 items. What is the profit or loss percent?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of each article be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 30 items = ₹ 30, S.P. of 30 items = ₹ 25.
Loss = ₹ (30 – 25) = ₹ 5.
Loss% = $\left(\frac{5}{30}\times100\right)$ % = 16 $\frac{2}{3}$ %.

questions.question_text: By selling 33 metres of cloth, one gains the selling price of 11 metres. Find the gain percent.
questions.solution:
(S.P. of 33 m) – (C.P. of 33 m) = Gain = S.P. of 11 m.
S.P. of 22 m = C.P. of 33 m.
Let C.P. of each metre be ₹ 1. Then, C.P. of 22 m = ₹ 22, S.P. of 22 m = ₹ 33.
Gain% = $\left(\frac{11}{22}\times100\right)$ % = 50%.

questions.question_text: A vendor bought bananas at 6 for ₹ 10 and sold them at 4 for ₹ 6. Find his gain or loss percent.
questions.solution:
Suppose, number of bananas bought = L.C.M. of 6 and 4 = 12.
C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{10}{6}\times12\right)$ = ₹ 20; S.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{6}{4}\times12\right)$ = ₹ 18.
Loss% = $\left(\frac{2}{20}\times100\right)$ % = 10%.

questions.question_text: A vendor sells 10 clips for a rupee gaining thereby 40%. How many clips did he buy for a rupee?
questions.solution:
S.P. of 10 clips = ₹ 1, Gain = 40%. C.P. of 10 clips = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{140}\times1\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{5}{7}$.
For ₹ $\frac{5}{7}$, clips bought = 10. For ₹ 1, clips bought = $\left(10\times\frac{7}{5}\right)$ = 14.

questions.question_text: A vendor bought buttons at 6 for a rupee. How many for a rupee must he sell to gain 20%?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 6 buttons = ₹ 1, Gain = 20%. S.P. of 6 buttons = ₹ $\left(\frac{120}{100}\times1\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{6}{5}$.
For ₹ $\frac{6}{5}$, buttons sold = 6. For ₹ 1, buttons sold = $\left(6\times\frac{5}{6}\right)$ = 5.

questions.question_text: A grocer purchased 80 kg of sugar at ₹ 13.50 per kg and mixed it with 120 kg sugar at ₹ 16 per kg. At what rate should he sell the mixture to gain 16%?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 200 kg of mixture = ₹ (80 × 13.50 + 120 × 16) = ₹ 3000.
S.P. = 116% of ₹ 3000 = ₹ $\left(\frac{116}{100}\times3000\right)$ = ₹ 3480.
Rate of S.P. of the mixture = ₹ $\left(\frac{3480}{200}\right)$ per kg = ₹ 17.40 per kg.

questions.question_text: Pure ghee costs ₹ 100 per kg. After adulterating it with vegetable oil costing ₹ 50 per kg, a shopkeeper sells the mixture at the rate of ₹ 96 per kg, thereby making a profit of 20%. In what ratio does he mix the two?
questions.solution:
Mean cost price = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{120}\times96\right)$ = ₹ 80 per kg.
By the rule of alligation:
C.P. of 1kg ghee (100) - Mean price (80) = 20
C.P. of 1kg oil (50) - Mean price (80) = 30
Required ratio = 30 : 20 = 3 : 2.

questions.question_text: A dishonest dealer professes to sell his goods at cost price but uses a weight of 960 gms for a kg. weight. Find his gain percent.
questions.solution: Gain% = $\left[\frac{\text{Error}}{\text{(True Value) - (Error)}}\times100\right]$ % = $\left(\frac{40}{960}\times100\right)$ % = 4 $\frac{1}{6}$ %.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper advertises for selling cloth at 4% loss. However, by using a false metre scale he actually gains 25%. What is the actual length of the scale?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of 1 m of cloth be ₹ 1.
Professed S.P. of 1 m cloth = ₹ $\left(\frac{96}{100}\right)$ = ₹ 0.96. Gain = 25%.
Actual C.P. of the cloth sold for ₹ 0.96 = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{125}\times0.96\right)$ = ₹ 0.768.
Actual length of the scale = Length bought for ₹ 0.768 = 76.8 cm.

questions.question_text: A dishonest dealer sells the goods at 6 $\frac{1}{4}$% loss on cost price but uses 12 $\frac{1}{2}$% less weight. What is his percentage profit or loss?
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. of 1 kg goods be ₹ 1.
Then, S.P. of $\left(100 - 12\frac{1}{2}\right)$% of 1 kg i.e., 875 g goods = ₹ 0.9375.
S.P. of 1 kg goods = ₹ $\left(\frac{0.9375}{875}\times1000\right)$ = ₹ 1 $\frac{1}{14}$.
Profit % = $\left(\frac{1}{14}\times100\right)$ % = 7 $\frac{1}{7}$ %.

questions.question_text: If the manufacturer gains 10%, the wholesale dealer 15% and the retailer 25%, then find the cost of production of a table, the retail price of which is ₹ 1265?
questions.solution:
Let the cost of production of the table be ₹ x.
Then, 125% of 115% of 110% of x = 1265
⇒ $\left(\frac{125}{100}\times\frac{115}{100}\times\frac{110}{100}\times x\right)$ = 1265
⇒ $\left(\frac{253}{160}\times x\right)$ = 1265
⇒ x = $\left(\frac{1265\times160}{253}\right)$ = ₹ 800.

questions.question_text: Monika purchased a pressure cooker at $\frac{9}{10}$ th of its selling price and sold it at 8% more than its S.P. Find her gain percent.
questions.solution:
Let the S.P. be ₹ x. Then, C.P. = ₹ $\frac{9x}{10}$, Receipt = 108% of ₹ x = ₹ $\frac{27x}{25}$.
Gain = ₹ $\left(\frac{27x}{25} - \frac{9x}{10}\right)$ = ₹ $\left(\frac{108x - 90x}{100}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{18x}{100}$.
Gain% = $\left(\frac{18x}{100}\times\frac{10}{9x}\times100\right)$ % = 20%.

questions.question_text: An article is sold at a certain price. By selling it at $\frac{2}{3}$ of that price one loses 10%. Find the gain percent at original price.
questions.solution:
Let the original S.P. be ₹ x. Then, New S.P. = ₹ $\frac{2x}{3}$, Loss = 10%.
So, C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{90}\times\frac{2x}{3}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{20x}{27}$.
Now, C.P. = ₹ $\frac{20x}{27}$, S.P. = ₹ x. Gain = ₹ $\left(x - \frac{20x}{27}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{7x}{27}$.
Gain% = $\left(\frac{7x}{27}\times\frac{27}{20x}\times100\right)$ % = 35%.

questions.question_text: A tradesman sold an article at a loss of 20%. If the selling price had been increased by ₹ 100, there would have been a gain of 5%. What was the cost price of the article?
A man sells an article at a profit of 25%. If he had bought it at 20% less and sold it for ₹ 10.50 less, he would have gained 30%. Find the cost price of the article.
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. be ₹ x.
1st S.P. = 125% of x = $\left(\frac{125}{100}\times x\right)$ = $\frac{5x}{4}$; 2nd C.P. = 80% of x = $\left(\frac{80}{100}\times x\right)$ = $\frac{4x}{5}$.
2nd S.P. = 130% of $\frac{4x}{5}$ = $\left(\frac{130}{100}\times\frac{4x}{5}\right)$ = $\frac{26x}{25}$.
∴ $\left(\frac{5x}{4} - \frac{26x}{25}\right)$ = 10.50 ⇔ $\left(\frac{21x}{100}\right)$ = 10.50 ⇔ x = $\left(\frac{10.50\times100}{21}\right)$ = 50.
Hence, C.P. = ₹ 50.

questions.question_text: The price of a jewel, passing through three hands, rises on the whole by 65%. If the first and the second sellers earned 20% and 25% profit respectively, find the percentage profit earned by the third seller.
questions.solution:
Let the original price of the jewel be ₹ P and let the profit earned by the third seller be x%.
Then, (100 + x)% of 125% of 120% of P = 165% of P
⇒ $\left[\left(\frac{100+x}{100}\right)\times\frac{125}{100}\times\frac{120}{100}\times P\right]$ = $\left(\frac{165}{100}\times P\right)$
⇒ (100 + x) = $\left(\frac{165\times100\times100}{125\times120}\right)$ = 110 ⇒ x = 10%.

questions.question_text: A man sold two flats for ₹ 6,75,958 each. On one he gains 16% while on the other he loses 16%. How much does he gain or lose in the whole transaction?
questions.solution:
Remember: In such a case, there is always a loss. The selling price is immaterial.
$\therefore$ Loss% = $\left(\frac{\text{Common Loss and Gain}}{10}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{16}{10}\right)^2\% = \left(\frac{64}{25}\right)\% = 2.56\%$.

questions.question_text: Two-thirds of a consignment was sold at a profit of 5% and the remainder at a loss of 2%. If the total profit was ₹ 400, find the value of the consignment.
questions.solution:
Let the value of the consignment be ₹ $x$.
C.P. of $\frac{2}{3}$rd = ₹ $\frac{2x}{3}$; C.P. of $\frac{1}{3}$rd = ₹ $\frac{x}{3}$.
Total S.P. = ₹ $\left[105\% \text{ of } \frac{2x}{3} + 98\% \text{ of } \frac{x}{3}\right] = ₹ \left(\frac{7x}{10} + \frac{49x}{150}\right) = ₹ \left(\frac{154x}{150}\right) = ₹ \frac{77x}{75}$.
Gain = ₹ $\left(\frac{77x}{75} - x\right) = ₹ \frac{2x}{75}$.
$\therefore \frac{2x}{75} = 400 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{400 \times 75}{2}\right) = 15000$.
Hence, value of the consignment = ₹ 15000.

questions.question_text: A man bought a horse and a carriage for ₹ 3000. He sold the horse at a gain of 20% and the carriage at a loss of 10%, thereby gaining 2% on the whole. Find the cost of the horse.
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. of the horse be ₹ $x$. Then, C.P. of the carriage = ₹ $(3000 - x)$.
$\therefore 20\% \text{ of } x - 10\% \text{ of } (3000 - x) = 2\% \text{ of } 3000$
$\Rightarrow \frac{20x}{100} - \frac{10(3000 - x)}{100} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{100}$
$\Rightarrow 20x - 30000 + 10x = 6000$
$\Rightarrow 30x = 36000 \Rightarrow x = 1200$.
Hence, C.P. of the horse = ₹ 1200.

questions.question_text: Find the single discount equivalent to a series discount of 10%, 20% and 30%.
questions.solution:
Let marked price be ₹ 100.
Then, Net S.P. = (100 - 30)% of (100 - 20)% of (100 - 10)% of ₹ 100
= 70% of 80% of 90% of ₹ 100
= ₹ $\left(\frac{70}{100} \times \frac{80}{100} \times \frac{90}{100} \times 100\right) = ₹ 50.40$.
$\therefore$ Required discount = (100 - 50.40)% = 49.6%.

questions.question_text: After getting two successive discounts, a shirt with a list price of ₹ 150 is available at ₹ 105. If the second discount is 12.5%, find the first discount.
questions.solution:
Let the first discount be $x\%$.
Then, 87.5% of (100 - $x$)% of 150 = 105
$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{87.5}{100} \times \frac{(100 - x)}{100} \times 150\right) = 105$
$\Rightarrow (100 - x) = \left(\frac{105 \times 100 \times 100}{150 \times 87.5}\right) = 80$
$\Rightarrow x = (100 - 80) = 20$.
$\therefore$ First discount = 20%.

questions.question_text: An uneducated retailer marks all his goods at 50% above the cost price and thinking that he will still make 25% profit, offers a discount of 25% on the marked price. What is his actual profit on the sales?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ 100. Then, marked price = ₹ 150.
S.P. = 75% of ₹ 150 = ₹ 112.50.
$\therefore$ Gain% = 12.50%.

questions.question_text: A retailer buys 40 pens at the marked price of 36 pens from a wholesaler. If he sells these pens giving a discount of 1%, what is the profit percent?
questions.solution:
Let the marked price of each pen be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 40 pens = ₹ 36. S.P. of 40 pens = 99% of ₹ 40 = ₹ 39.60.
$\therefore$ Profit % = $\left(\frac{3.60}{36} \times 100\right) % = 10\%$.

questions.question_text: By how much above the cost should the goods be marked for sale so that after allowing a trade discount of 20% and a cash discount of $6\frac{1}{4}\%$, a net gain of 20% on the cost is made?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ 100. Then, S.P. = ₹ 120.
Let M.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $\left(100 - 6\frac{1}{4}\right)\%$ of (100 - 20)% of $x = 120$
$\Rightarrow 93\frac{3}{4}\%$ of 80% of $x = 120$
$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{375}{400} \times \frac{80}{100} \times x\right) = 120 \Rightarrow \frac{3}{4}x = 120 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{120 \times 4}{3}\right) = 160$.
$\therefore$ Marked price = 60% above C.P.

questions.question_text: When a producer allows 36% commission on the retail price of his product, he earns a profit of 8.8%. What would be his profit percent if the commission is reduced by 24%?
questions.solution:
Let retail price = ₹ 100. Then, commission = ₹ 36.
$\therefore$ S.P. = ₹ (100 - 36) = ₹ 64. But, profit = 8.8%.
$\therefore$ C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{108.8} \times 64\right) = ₹ \frac{1000}{17}$.
New commission = ₹ 12. New S.P. = ₹ (100 - 12) = ₹ 88.
Gain = ₹ $\left(88 - \frac{1000}{17}\right) = ₹ \frac{496}{17}$.
$\therefore$ Gain% = $\left(\frac{496}{17} \times \frac{17}{1000} \times 100\right) % = 49.6\%$.

questions.question_text: Mr Kashyap purchased an airconditioner for ₹ 12000 and sold it for ₹ 15000. What was the profit percentage?

questions.passage_text:
In terms of percentage profit, which is the best transaction?
C.P. (in ₹) Profit (in ₹)
(a) 36 17
(b) 50 24
(c) 40 19
(d) 60 29
questions.question_text: In terms of percentage profit, which is the best transaction?
questions.solution:
(a) Profit % = $\left(\frac{17}{36}\times100\right)$% = $47\frac{2}{9}$%.
(b) Profit % = $\left(\frac{24}{50}\times100\right)$% = 48%.
(c) Profit % = $\left(\frac{19}{40}\times100\right)$% = $47\frac{1}{2}$%.
(d) Profit % = $\left(\frac{29}{60}\times100\right)$% = $48\frac{1}{3}$%.
Clearly, (d) is the best transaction.

questions.question_text: If books bought at prices ranging from ₹ 200 to ₹ 350 are sold at prices ranging from ₹ 300 to ₹ 425, what is the greatest possible profit that might be made in selling eight books?
questions.solution:
Least C.P. = ₹ (200 $\times$ 8) = ₹ 1600.
Greatest S.P. = ₹ (425 $\times$ 8) = ₹ 3400.
Required profit = ₹ (3400 – 1600) = ₹ 1800.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper bought an article for ₹ 2090.42. Approximately, what will be the percentage profit if he sold that article for ₹ 2602.58?
The cost price of an article is ₹ 7840. What should be the selling price of the article so that there is a profit of 7%?
questions.solution:
C.P. = ₹ 7840, Profit = 7%.
$\therefore$ S.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{107}{100}\times7840\right)$ = ₹ 8388.80.

questions.question_text: Rakesh purchased a mobile phone for ₹ 5400 and a refrigerator for ₹ 9600. He sold the mobile phone at three-fourths of its cost price and the refrigerator at $1\frac{1}{3}$ of its cost price. What was the profit/loss?

Rajni purchased a mobile phone and a refrigerator for ₹ 12000 and ₹ 10000 respectively. She sold the refrigerator at a loss of 12 percent and the mobile phone at a profit of 8 percent. What is her overall loss/profit?
Mohanlal purchased a TV set for ₹ 12500 and spent ₹ 300 on transportation and ₹ 800 on installation. At what price should he sell it so as to earn an overall profit of 15%?
Harshad bought 15 pieces of DVD players @ ₹ 4500 each and sold all of them at the total price of ₹ 81000. What is the percent profit earned in the deal?
Alfred buys an old scooter for ₹ 4700 and spends ₹ 800 on its repairs. If he sells the scooter for ₹ 5800, his gain percent is
A shopkeeper purchased 70 kg of potatoes for ₹ 420 and sold the whole lot at the rate of ₹ 6.50 per kg. What will be his gain percent?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 1 kg = $\frac{420}{70}$ = ₹ 6. S.P. of 1 kg = ₹ 6.50.
$\therefore$ Gain % = $\left(\frac{0.50}{6}\times100\right)$% = $\frac{25}{3}$% = $8\frac{1}{3}$%.

questions.question_text: Sam purchased 20 dozens of toys at the rate of ₹ 375 per dozen. He sold each one of them at the rate of ₹ 33. What was his percentage profit?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 1 toy = $\frac{375}{12}$ = ₹ 31.25. S.P. of 1 toy = ₹ 33.
$\therefore$ Profit % = $\left(\frac{1.75}{31.25}\times100\right)$% = $\frac{28}{5}$% = 5.6%.

questions.question_text: 100 oranges are bought at the rate of ₹ 350 and sold at the rate of ₹ 48 per dozen. The percentage of profit or loss is
questions.solution:
C.P. of 1 orange = $\frac{350}{100}$ = ₹ 3.50. S.P. of 1 orange
= $\frac{48}{12}$ = ₹ 4.
$\therefore$ Gain % = $\left(\frac{0.50}{3.50}\times100\right)$% = $\frac{100}{7}$% = $14\frac{2}{7}$%.

questions.question_text: A sells an article which costs him ₹ 400 to B at a profit of 20%. B then sells it to C, making a profit of 10% on the price he paid to A. How much does C pay B?
questions.solution:
C.P. for B = 120% of ₹ 400 = $\left(\frac{120}{100}\times400\right)$ = ₹ 480.
C.P. for C = 110% of ₹ 480 = $\left(\frac{110}{100}\times480\right)$ = ₹ 528.

questions.question_text: By selling an article for ₹ 100, a man gains ₹ 15. Then, his gain % is
A trader buys some goods for ₹ 150. If the overhead expenses be 12% of cost price, then at what price should it be sold to earn 10%?
questions.solution:
Total C.P. = Cost + Overhead expenses = ₹ (150 + 12% of 150) = ₹ (150 + 18) = ₹ 168.
$\therefore$ S.P. = $\left(\frac{110}{100}\times168\right)$ = ₹ 184.80.

questions.question_text: A man buys 10 articles for ₹ 8 and sells them at the rate of ₹ 1.25 per article. His profit is
questions.solution:
C.P. of 10 articles = ₹ 8. S.P. of 10 articles
= ₹ (1.25 $\times$ 10) = ₹ 12.5.
Profit = ₹ (12.5 – 8) = ₹ 4.50.
$\therefore$ Profit % = $\left(\frac{4.5}{8}\times100\right)$% = $56\frac{1}{4}$%.

questions.question_text: If an article is sold at 200 percent profit, then the ratio of its cost price to its selling price will be
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ x. Profit = 200%.
$\therefore$ S.P. = 300% of ₹ x = ₹ 3x.
Required ratio = x : 3x = 1 : 3.

questions.question_text: If the ratio of cost price and selling price of an article be 10 : 11, the percentage of profit is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ 10x and S.P. = ₹ 11x.
Profit = ₹ (11x – 10x) = ₹ x.
$\therefore$ Profit % = $\left(\frac{x}{10x}\times100\right)$% = 10%.

questions.question_text: A trader sells an article and loses $12\frac{1}{2}\%$. The ratio of cost price to the selling price is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ x. Loss = $12\frac{1}{2}$%.
S.P. = $87\frac{1}{2}$% of ₹ x = ₹ $\left(\frac{175}{2}\times\frac{1}{100}\times x\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{7x}{8}$.
$\therefore$ Required ratio = x : $\frac{7x}{8}$ = 8 : 7.

questions.question_text: A person buys an article for ₹ $p$ and sells it for ₹ $q$ thereby gaining $r\%$. The selling price in terms of cost price may be written as
The owner of a furniture shop charges his customer 28% more than the cost price. If a customer paid ₹ 23680 for a dining table set, then what was the original price of the dining set?
A gold bracelet is sold for ₹ 14500 at a loss of 20%. What is the cost price of the gold bracelet?
A shopkeeper expects a gain of $22\frac{1}{2}$% on his cost price. If in a week, his sale was of ₹ 392, what was his profit?
The sale price of an article including the sales tax is ₹ 616. The rate of sales tax is 10%. If the shopkeeper has made a profit of 12%, then the cost price of the article is
questions.solution:
$110\%$ of S.P. = $616$ $\Rightarrow$ S.P. = ₹ $\left(616\times\frac{100}{110}\right)$ = ₹ $560$.
$\therefore$ C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{112}\times560\right)$ = ₹ $500$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper buys 144 eggs at 90 paise each. On the way 20 eggs were broken. He sold the remaining eggs at ₹ 1.20 each. The percentage gain or loss is

Abhishek purchased 140 shirts and 250 trousers @ ₹ 450 and @ ₹ 550 respectively. What should be the overall average selling price of shirts and trousers so that 40% profit is earned? (rounded off to next integer)
A person purchased 10 dozen pens at the rate of ₹ 4 per dozen. On checking, he found that 20 pens were not working. In order to earn 25% profit, he should sell the remaining pens each at
questions.solution:
Total C.P. of $(10\times12)$ i.e., $120$ pens = ₹ $(4\times10)$ = ₹ $40$.
Number of working pens = $(120 - 20) = 100$.
Total S.P. of $100$ pens = ₹ $\left(\frac{125}{100}\times40\right)$ = ₹ $50$.
$\therefore$ S.P. of each pen = ₹ $\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)$ = $50$ paise.

questions.question_text: Saransh purchased 120 reams of paper at ₹ 80 per ream. He spent ₹ 280 on transportation, paid octroi at the rate of 40 paise per ream and paid ₹ 72 to the coolie. If he wants to have a gain of 8%, what must be the selling price per ream?

A person bought 20 litres of milk at the rate of ₹ 8 per litre. He got it churned after spending ₹ 10 and 5 kg of cream and 20 litres of toned milk were obtained. If he sold the cream at ₹ 30 per kg and toned milk at ₹ 4 per litre, his profit in the transaction is
questions.solution:
Investment = ₹ $(20\times8 + 10)$ = ₹ $170$.
Receipt = ₹ $(30\times5 + 20\times4)$ = ₹ $230$.
$\therefore$ Gain $\%$ = $\left(\frac{60}{170}\times100\right)\%$ = $35.29\% \approx 35.3\%$.

questions.question_text: Jacob bought a scooter for a certain sum of money. He spent 10% of the cost on repairs and sold the scooter for a profit of ₹ 1100. How much did he spend on repairs if he made a profit of 20%?
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $20\%$ of $x = 1100$ $\Rightarrow$ $\frac{20}{100}\times x = 1100$ $\Rightarrow$ $x = 5500$.
C.P. = ₹ $5500$, Expenditure on repairs = $10\%$.
Actual price = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{110}\times5500\right)$ = ₹ $5000$.
$\therefore$ Expenditure on repairs = ₹ $(5500 - 5000)$ = ₹ $500$.

questions.question_text: A manufacturer undertakes to supply 2000 pieces of a particular component at ₹ 25 per piece. According to his estimates, even if 5% fail to pass the quality tests, then he will make a profit of 25%. However, as it turned out, 50% of the components were rejected. What is the loss to the manufacturer?
questions.solution:
Total cost incurred = ₹ $\left[\frac{100}{125}\times25\times(95\% \text{ of } 2000)\right]$
= ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{125}\times25\times1900\right)$ = ₹ $38000$.
Loss to the manufacturer = ₹ $[38000 - (25\times1000)]$
= ₹ $13000$.

questions.question_text: Ronit and Vinit purchased a scooter for ₹ 25000 and sold the same for ₹ 26250. If at the time of purchase Ronit paid $1\frac{1}{2}$ times as much as Vinit, how much did Vinit receive out of profit?
questions.solution:
Ratio of profit = Ratio of investments = $3:1$ = $3:2$.
Profit = ₹ $(26250 - 25000)$ = ₹ $1250$.
$\therefore$ Vinit’s share = ₹ $\left(\frac{2}{5}\times1250\right)$ = ₹ $500$.

questions.question_text: A trader buys a chair for ₹ 600 and sells it for ₹ 765 at a credit of 4 months. Reckoning money worth 6% p.a., his gain percent is
By selling a bicycle for ₹ 2850, a shopkeeper gains 14%. If the profit is reduced to 8% then the selling price will be
questions.solution:
Let the new S.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $114 : 2850 = 108 : x$ $\Rightarrow$ $x = \left(\frac{2850\times108}{114}\right)$ = $2700$.

questions.question_text: When a plot is sold for ₹ 18,700, the owner loses 15%. At what price must the plot be sold in order to gain 15%?
questions.solution:
Let the new S.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $85 : 18700 = 115 : x$ $\Rightarrow$ $x = \left(\frac{18700\times115}{85}\right)$ = $25300$.

questions.question_text: A fruitseller sells mangoes at the rate of ₹ 9 per kg and thereby loses 20%. At what price per kg, he should have sold them to make a profit of 5%?
questions.solution:
Let the new S.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $80 : 9 = 105 : x$ $\Rightarrow$ $x = \left(\frac{9\times105}{80}\right)$ = $11.81$.

questions.question_text: Raju purchased an item for ₹ 8200 and sold it at a gain of 25%. From that amount he purchased another item and sold it at a loss of 20%. What is his overall gain/loss?
questions.solution:
Initial investment = ₹ $8200$.
S.P. of 1st item = ₹ $\left(\frac{125}{100}\times8200\right)$ = ₹ $10250$.
C.P. of 2nd item = ₹ $10250$. Loss = $20\%$.
Final receipt = S.P. of 2nd item = ₹ $\left(\frac{80}{100}\times10250\right)$
= ₹ $8200$.
Since initial investment = final receipt, there was neither gain nor loss.

questions.question_text: A property dealer sells a house for ₹ 6,30,000 and in the bargain makes a profit of 5%. Had he sold it for ₹ 5,00,000, then what percentage of loss or gain he would have made?
questions.solution:
C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{105}\times630000\right)$ = ₹ $600000$.
$\therefore$ Required loss% = $\left(\frac{100000}{600000}\times100\right)\%$ = $16\frac{2}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper sells one transistor for ₹ 840 at a gain of 20% and another for ₹ 960 at a loss of 4%. His total gain or loss percent is
questions.solution:
C.P. of 1st transistor = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{120}\times840\right)$ = ₹ $700$.
C.P. of 2nd transistor = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{96}\times960\right)$ = ₹ $1000$.
So, total C.P. = ₹ $(700 + 1000) = 1700$.
Total S.P. = ₹ $(840 + 960) = ₹ 1800$.
$\therefore$ Gain% = $\left(\frac{100}{1700}\times100\right)\%$ = $5\frac{15}{17}\%$.

questions.question_text: If selling price of an article is $\frac{4}{3}$ of its cost price, the profit in the transaction is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $x$. Then, S.P. = ₹ $ rac{4}{3}x$.
Gain = ₹ $ rac{4}{3}x - x =  rac{x}{3}$.
Gain% = $ rac{x}{3} 	imes  rac{1}{x} 	imes 100$ % = $33 rac{1}{3}$%.

questions.question_text: The ratio between the sale price and the cost price of an article is 7 : 5. What is the ratio between the profit and the cost price of that article?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $5x$ and S.P. = ₹ $7x$.
Then, Gain = ₹ $2x$.
Required ratio = $2x : 5x = 2 : 5$.

questions.question_text: If an article is sold for ₹ x, there is a loss of 15%. If the same article is sold for ₹ y, there is a profit of 15%. The ratio of (y – x) to (y + x) is
By selling an article at some price, a man gains 10%. If the article is sold at twice of the price, the gain percent will be
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $x$. Then, S.P. = 110% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $ rac{11x}{10}$.
New S.P. = ₹ $ rac{11x}{10} 	imes  rac{1}{2} =  rac{11x}{20}$. Gain = $ rac{11x}{20} - x =  rac{x}{20}$.
Gain% = $ rac{x}{20} 	imes  rac{1}{x} 	imes 100$ % = 5%. (Note: The solution in the book appears to have a typo in the calculation steps, but the logic follows the standard profit formula).

questions.question_text: If selling price is doubled, the profit triples. Find the profit percent.
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$ and S.P. be ₹ $y$.
Then, $3(y - x) = (2y - x) ightarrow y = 2x$.
Profit = ₹ $(y - x) = ₹ (2x - x) = ₹ x$.
Profit % = $ rac{x}{x} 	imes 100$ % = 100%.

questions.question_text: At what profit percent must an article be sold so that by selling at half that price, there may be a loss of 30%?
questions.solution:
Let S.P. = ₹ $x$. New S.P. = ₹ $ rac{x}{2}$, Loss = 30%.
So, C.P. = ₹ $ rac{100}{70} 	imes  rac{x}{2} = ₹  rac{5x}{7}$.
Profit = ₹ $ rac{x}{1} -  rac{5x}{7} = ₹  rac{2x}{7}$.
Profit% = $ rac{2x}{7} 	imes  rac{7}{5x} 	imes 100$ % = 40%.

questions.question_text: The C.P. of an article is 40% of the S.P. The percent that the S.P. is of C.P. is
questions.solution:
C.P. = $ rac{40}{100} 	imes$ S.P. $ightarrow$ S.P. = $ rac{5}{2}$ C.P.
C.P. = $ rac{5}{2} 	imes 100$ % of C.P. = 250% of C.P.

questions.question_text: By selling a pen for ₹ 15, a man loses one-sixteenth of what it costs him. The cost price of the pen is
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $x - 15 =  rac{15}{16}x ightarrow x -  rac{15}{16}x = 15 ightarrow  rac{x}{16} = 15 ightarrow x = 240$. (Note: The book solution states $x=16$, but the calculation $x - 15 =  rac{15}{16}x$ leads to $x=240$).

questions.question_text: By selling an article, Michael earned a profit equal to one-fourth of the price he bought it. If he sold it for ₹ 375, what was the cost price?
10% loss on selling price is what percent loss on the cost price?
If loss is $\frac{1}{3}$ of S.P., the loss percentage is
questions.solution:
Let S.P. = ₹ $x$. Then, Loss = ₹ $ rac{x}{3}$.
C.P. = ₹ $(x +  rac{x}{3}) = ₹  rac{4x}{3}$.
Loss% = $ rac{x/3}{4x/3} 	imes 100$ % = 25%.

questions.question_text: In a certain store, the profit is 320% of the cost. If the cost increases by 25% but the selling price remains constant, approximately what percentage of the selling price is the profit?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ 100. Then, Profit = ₹ 320, S.P. = ₹ 420.
New C.P. = 125% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 125; New S.P. = ₹ 420.
Profit = ₹ $(420 - 125) = ₹ 295$.
Required percentage = $ rac{295}{420} 	imes 100$ % $ightarrow$ $ rac{1475}{21}$% $ightarrow$ 70%.

questions.question_text: Ashok buys a car at 20% discount of the price and sells it at 20% higher price. His percentage gain is
The profit earned after selling an article for ₹ 1754 is the same as loss incurred after selling the article for ₹ 1492. What is the cost price of the article?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $x$. Then, $1754 - x = x - 1492$
$ightarrow 2x = 3246 ightarrow x = 1623$.

questions.question_text: The profit earned by selling an article for ₹ 832 is equal to the loss incurred when the same article is sold for ₹ 448. What should be the sale price for making 50% profit?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $x$. Then, $832 - x = x - 448$
$ightarrow 2x = 1280 ightarrow x = 640$.
Required S.P. = 150% of ₹ 640 = ₹ $ rac{150}{100} 	imes 640 = ₹ 960$.

questions.question_text: The profit earned by selling an article for ₹ 900 is double the loss incurred when the same article is sold for ₹ 450. At what price should the article be sold to make 25% profit?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $x$. Then, $900 - x = 2(x - 450)$
$ightarrow 3x = 1800 ightarrow x = 600$.
Required S.P. = 125% of ₹ 600 = ₹ $ rac{125}{100} 	imes 600 = ₹ 750$.

questions.question_text: The percentage profit earned by selling an article for ₹ 1920 is equal to the percentage loss incurred by selling the same article for ₹ 1280. At what price should the article be sold to make 25% profit?
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $ rac{1920 - x}{x} 	imes 100 =  rac{x - 1280}{x} 	imes 100$
$ightarrow 2x = 3200 ightarrow x = 1600$.
Required S.P. = 125% of ₹ 1600 = ₹ 2000.

questions.question_text: Profit earned by selling an article for ₹ 1060 is 20% more than the loss incurred by selling the article for ₹ 950. At what price should the article be sold to earn 20% profit?
When an article is sold for ₹ 116, the profit percent is thrice as much as when it is sold for ₹ 92. The cost price of the article is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $ rac{116 - x}{x} 	imes 100 = 3 	imes  rac{92 - x}{x} 	imes 100 ightarrow x = 80$.

questions.question_text: If the cost price of 15 books is equal to the selling price of 20 books, the loss percent is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of each book be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 20 books = ₹ 20; S.P. of 20 books = ₹ 15.
Loss% = $ rac{5}{20} 	imes 100$ % = 25%.

questions.question_text: If the cost price of 10 articles is equal to the selling price of 7 articles, then the gain or loss percent is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of each article be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 7 articles = ₹ 7; S.P. of 7 articles = ₹ 10.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{3}{7}\times100\right)\% = 42\frac{6}{7}\%$.

questions.question_text: A wholeseller buys 20 pens at the marked price of 16 pens to a retailer. The retailer in turn sells them at the marked price. Determine the gain or loss percent to the retailer.
questions.solution:
Let M.P. of each pen be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 20 pens = ₹ 16; S.P. of 20 pens = ₹ 20.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{4}{16}\times100\right)\% = 25\%$.

questions.question_text: A farmer bought 749 sheep. He sold 700 of them for the price paid for the 749 sheep. The remaining 49 sheep were sold at the same price per head as the other 700. Based on the cost, the percent gain on the entire transaction is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of each sheep be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 700 sheep = ₹ 700; S.P. of 700 sheep = ₹ 749.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{49}{700}\times100\right)\% = 7\%$.

questions.question_text: If by selling 110 mangoes, the C.P. of 120 mangoes is realised, the gain percent is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of each mango be ₹ 1.
C.P. of 110 mangoes = ₹ 110, S.P. of 110 mangoes = ₹ 120.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{10}{110}\times100\right)\% = 9\frac{1}{11}\%$.

questions.question_text: The cost price of 20 articles is the same as the selling price of x articles. If the profit is 25%, then the value of x is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of each article be ₹ 1. C.P. of x articles = ₹ x; S.P. of x articles = ₹ 20.
Profit = ₹ (20 - x).
\ $\left(\frac{20-x}{x}\times100\right) = 25 \Rightarrow 2000 - 100x = 25x$
$\Rightarrow 125x = 2000 \Rightarrow x = 16$.

questions.question_text: On an order of 5 dozen boxes of a consumer product, a retailer receives an extra dozen free. This is equivalent to allowing him a discount of
questions.solution:
Clearly, the retailer gets 1 dozen out of 6 dozens free.
\ Equivalent discount = $\left(\frac{1}{6}\times100\right)\% = 16\frac{2}{3}\%$.

questions.question_text: A man sold 18 cots for ₹ 16,800, gaining thereby the cost price of 3 cots. The cost price of a cot is
questions.solution:
(S.P. of 18 cots) - (C.P. of 18 cots) = (C.P. of 3 cots)
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 21 cots = S.P. of 18 cots = ₹ 16800
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 1 cot = ₹ $\left(\frac{16800}{21}\right) = ₹ 800$.

questions.question_text: Mohan bought 20 dining tables for ₹ 12000 and sold them at a profit equal to the selling price of 4 dining tables. The selling price of 1 dining table is
questions.solution:
(S.P. of 20 dining tables) - (C.P. of 20 dining tables) = S.P. of 4 dining tables
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 20 dining tables = S.P. of 16 dining tables = ₹ 12000
$\Rightarrow$ S.P. of 1 dining table = ₹ $\left(\frac{12000}{16}\right) = ₹ 750$.

questions.question_text: By selling 100 pencils, a shopkeeper gains the selling price of 20 pencils. His gain percent is
questions.solution:
(S.P. of 100 pencils) - (C.P. of 100 pencils) = S.P. of 20 pencils
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 100 pencils = S.P. of 80 pencils.
Let C.P. of each pencil be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of 80 pencils = ₹ 80; S.P. of 80 pencils = ₹ 100.
\ Gain % = $\left(\frac{20}{80}\times100\right)\% = 25\%$.

questions.question_text: On selling 17 balls at ₹ 720, there is a loss equal to the cost price of 5 balls. The cost price of a ball is
questions.solution:
(C.P. of 17 balls) - (S.P. of 17 balls) = (C.P. of 5 balls)
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 12 balls = S.P. of 17 balls = ₹ 720
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 1 ball = ₹ $\left(\frac{720}{12}\right) = ₹ 60$.

questions.question_text: A vendor loses the selling price of 4 oranges on selling 36 oranges. His loss percent is
questions.solution:
(C.P. of 36 mangoes) - (S.P. of 36 mangoes) = Loss = (S.P. of 4 mangoes)
$\Rightarrow$ S.P. of 40 mangoes = C.P. of 36 mangoes.
Let C.P. of each mango be ₹ 1.
C.P. of 40 mangoes = ₹ 40; S.P. of 40 mangoes = ₹ 36.
\ Loss% = $\left(\frac{4}{40}\times100\right)\% = 10\%$.

questions.question_text: A man buys 2 dozen bananas at ₹ 16 per dozen. After selling 18 bananas at the rate of ₹ 12 per dozen, the shopkeeper reduced the rate to ₹ 4 per dozen. The percent loss is
A man bought apples at the rate of 8 for ₹ 34 and sold them at the rate of 12 for ₹ 57. How many apples should be sold to earn a net profit of ₹ 45?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 1 apple = ₹ $\left(\frac{34}{8}\right) = ₹ 4.25$. S.P. of 1 apple = ₹ $\left(\frac{57}{12}\right) = ₹ 4.75$.
Profit on each apple = ₹ 0.50.
\ Number of apples required = $\left(\frac{45}{0.50}\right) = 90$.

questions.question_text: Vinod makes a profit of ₹ 110 if he sells a certain number of pencils he has at the price of ₹ 2.50 per pencil and incurs a loss of ₹ 55 if he sells the same number of pencils for ₹ 1.75 per pencil. How many pencils does Vinod have?
questions.solution:
Suppose Vinod has n pencils.
Then, 2.50 n - 1.75 n = 110 + 55
$\Rightarrow$ 0.75 n = 165 $\Rightarrow$ n = $\left(\frac{165}{0.75}\right) = 220$.

questions.question_text: Ram bought 1600 eggs at ₹ 3.75 a dozen. He sold 900 of them at 2 for ₹ 1 and the remaining at 5 for ₹ 2. His percent gain or loss is
questions.solution:
C.P. of 1600 eggs = ₹ $\left(\frac{3.75}{12}\times1600\right) = ₹ 500$.
S.P. of 1600 eggs = ₹ $\left(\frac{900}{12} + \frac{700}{25}\right) = ₹ 730$.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{230}{500}\times100\right)\% = 46\%$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper purchases 11 knives in ₹ 10 and sells them at the rate of 10 knives for ₹ 11. He earns a profit of
questions.solution:
Suppose, number of knives bought = L.C.M. of 11 and 10 = 110.
C.P. of 110 knives = ₹ $\left(\frac{10}{11}\times110\right) = ₹ 100$.
S.P. of 110 knives = ₹ $\left(\frac{11}{10}\times110\right) = ₹ 121$.
\ Profit% = $\left(\frac{21}{100}\times100\right)\% = 21\%$.

questions.question_text: Oranges are bought at 5 for ₹ 10 and sold at 6 for ₹ 15. The profit or loss as percentage is
questions.solution:
Suppose, number of oranges bought = L.C.M. of 5 and 6 = 30.
C.P. of 30 oranges = ₹ $\left(\frac{10}{5}\times30\right) = ₹ 60$.
S.P. of 30 oranges = ₹ $\left(\frac{15}{6}\times30\right) = ₹ 75$.
\ Profit% = $\left(\frac{15}{60}\times100\right)\% = 25\%$.

questions.question_text: A fruit seller buys lemons at 2 for a rupee and sells them at 5 for three rupees. His profit percent is
questions.solution:
Suppose number of lemons bought = L.C.M. of 2 and 5 = 10.
C.P. of 10 lemons = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{2}\times10\right) = ₹ 5$.
S.P. of 10 lemons = ₹ $\left(\frac{3}{5}\times10\right) = ₹ 6$.
\ Profit% = $\left(\frac{1}{5}\times100\right)\% = 20\%$.

questions.question_text: A man bought pencils at the rate of 6 for ₹ 4 and sold them at the rate of 4 for ₹ 6. His gain in the transaction is
questions.solution:
Suppose number of pencils bought = L.C.M. of 6 and 4 = 12.
C.P. of 12 pencils = ₹ $\left(\frac{4}{6}\times12\right) = ₹ 8$.
S.P. of 12 pencils = ₹ $\left(\frac{6}{4}\times12\right) = ₹ 18$.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{10}{8}\times100\right)\% = 125\%$.

questions.question_text: A man purchased a box full of pencils at the rate of 7 for ₹ 9 and sold all of them at the rate of 8 for ₹ 11. In this transaction, he gained ₹ 10. How many pencils did the box contain?
questions.solution:
Suppose, number of pencils bought = L.C.M. of 7 and 8 = 56.
C.P. of 56 pencils = ₹ $\left(\frac{9}{7}\times56\right) = ₹ 72$.
S.P. of 56 pencils = ₹ $\left(\frac{11}{8}\times56\right) = ₹ 77$.
Now, ₹ 5 are gained on 56 pencils.
So, ₹ 10 are gained on $\left(\frac{56}{5}\times10\right) = 112$ pencils.

questions.question_text: A man bought a number of clips at 3 for a rupee and an equal number at 2 for a rupee. At what price per dozen should he sell them to make a profit of 20%?
questions.solution:
Suppose he bought 1 dozen clips of each kind.
C.P. of 2 dozens = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{3}\times12+\frac{1}{2}\times12\right) = ₹ 10$.
S.P. of 2 dozens = 120% of ₹ 10 = ₹ $\left(\frac{120}{100}\times10\right) = ₹ 12$.
Hence, S.P. per dozen = ₹ 6.

questions.question_text: A man buys eggs at 2 for ₹ 1 and an equal number at 3 for ₹ 2 and sells the whole at 5 for ₹ 3. His gain or loss percent is
questions.solution:
Suppose he buys 6 eggs of each kind.
C.P. of 12 eggs = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{2}\times6+\frac{2}{3}\times6\right)$
= ₹ 7. S.P. of 12 eggs = ₹ $\left(\frac{3}{5}\times12\right) = ₹ 7.20$.
Gain = $\left(\frac{0.20}{7}\times100\right)\% = 2\frac{6}{7}\%$.

questions.question_text: A person buys certain number of marbles at 20 per rupee and an equal number at 30 per rupee. He mixes them and sells them at 25 per rupee. His gain or loss in the transaction is
questions.solution:
Suppose number of marbles bought of each kind = L.C.M. of 20, 30, 25 = 300.
C.P. of 600 marbles = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{20}\times300+\frac{1}{30}\times300\right) = ₹ 25$.
S.P. of 600 marbles = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{25}\times600\right) = ₹ 24$.
Loss% = $\left(\frac{1}{25}\times100\right)\% = 4\%$.

questions.question_text: A man bought some oranges at ₹ 10 per dozen and bought the same number of oranges at ₹ 8 per dozen. He sold these oranges at ₹ 11 per dozen and gained ₹ 120. The total number of oranges bought by him was
questions.solution:
C.P. of 2 dozen oranges = ₹ (10 + 8) = ₹ 18.
S.P. of 2 dozen oranges = ₹ 22.
If profit is ₹ 4, oranges bought = 2 dozen.
If profit is ₹ 120, oranges bought = $\left(\frac{2}{4}\times120\right)$ dozens = 60 dozens.

questions.question_text: A person bought some articles at the rate of 5 per rupee and the same number at the rate of 4 per rupee. He mixed both the types and sold at the rate of 9 for 2 rupees. In this business he suffered a loss of ₹ 3. The total number of articles bought by him was
questions.solution:
Suppose number of articles bought of each kind = L.C.M. of 5, 4, 9 = 180.
C.P. of 360 articles = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{5}\times180+\frac{1}{4}\times180\right) = ₹ 81$.
S.P. of 360 articles = ₹ $\left(\frac{2}{9}\times360\right) = ₹ 80$.
If loss is ₹ 1, articles bought = 360.
If loss is ₹ 3, articles bought = (360 $\times$ 3) = 1080.

questions.question_text: A grocer purchases three qualities of lemons at different rates. The first quality was purchased at 2 for ₹ 1, the second at 3 for ₹ 2 and the third at 4 for ₹ 3. He sold all the lemons at 5 for ₹ 4. If the ratio of the number of lemons of the three qualities is 1 : 2 : 3, then what is the approximate gain or loss percentage incurred by the grocer?
questions.solution:
L.C.M. of 2, 3, 4, 5 = 60.
Suppose the grocer purchased (60 $\times$ 1), (60 $\times$ 2), (60 $\times$ 3) lemons i.e., 60, 120 and 180 lemons of first, second and third qualities respectively.
Then, C.P. of 60 lemons of first quality = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{2}\times60\right) = ₹ 30$.
C.P. of 120 lemons of second quality = ₹ $\left(\frac{2}{3}\times120\right) = ₹ 80$.
C.P. of 180 lemons of third quality = ₹ $\left(\frac{3}{4}\times180\right) = ₹ 135$.
Total C.P. of (60 + 120 + 180) i.e., 360 lemons = ₹ (30 + 80 + 135) = ₹ 245.
S.P. of 360 lemons = ₹ $\left(\frac{4}{5}\times360\right) = ₹ 288$.
Gain = ₹ (288 - 245) = ₹ 43.
Gain% = $\left(\frac{43}{245}\times100\right)\% = 17.56\%$.

questions.question_text: A vendor bought toffees at 6 for a rupee. How many for a rupee must he sell to gain 20%?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 6 toffees = ₹ 1. S.P. of 6 toffees = 120% of ₹ 1 = ₹ $\frac{6}{5}$.
For ₹ $\frac{6}{5}$, toffees sold = 6. For ₹ 1, toffees sold = $\left(6\times\frac{5}{6}\right) = 5$.

questions.question_text: Reynolds Superink pens are bought at the rate of 8 for ₹ 100. To make a profit of 60 per cent, these must be sold at
questions.solution:
C.P. of 8 pens = ₹ 100. S.P. of 8 pens = 160% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 160.
For ₹ 160, pens sold = 8.
For ₹ 100, pens sold = $\left(\frac{8}{160}\times100\right) = 5$.

questions.question_text: By selling 12 toffees for a rupee, a man loses 20%. How many for a rupee should he sell to get a gain of 20%?
questions.solution:
Let S.P. of 12 toffees be ₹ x.
Then, 80 : 1 = 120 : x or x = $\left(\frac{120}{80}\right) = \frac{3}{2}$.

questions.question_text: By selling 45 lemons for ₹ 40, a man loses 20%. How many should he sell for ₹ 24 to gain 20% in the transaction?
questions.solution:
For ₹ $\frac{3}{2}$, toffees sold = 12.
For ₹ 1, toffees sold = $\left(12\times\frac{2}{3}\right) = 8$.
Let S.P. of 45 lemons be ₹ x.
Then, 80 : 40 = 120 : x or x = $\left(\frac{120\times40}{80}\right) = 60$.
For ₹ 60, lemons sold = 45.
For ₹ 24, lemons sold = $\left(\frac{45}{60}\times24\right) = 18$.

questions.question_text: By selling 90 ball pens for ₹ 160, a person loses 20%. How many ball pens should be sold for ₹ 96 so as to have a profit of 20%?
questions.solution:
S.P. of 90 ball pens = ₹ 160, Loss = 20%.
C.P. of 90 ball pens = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{80}\times160\right) = ₹ 200$.
Desired S.P. of 90 ball pens = ₹ $\left(\frac{120}{100}\times200\right) = ₹ 240$.
For ₹ 240, ball pens sold = 90.
For ₹ 96, ball pens sold = $\left(\frac{90}{240}\times96\right) = 36$.

questions.passage_text: Directions (Questions 92 to 96): Read the following information carefully and answer the questions that follow : A train journey from P to D by an X- express has 4 classes of fares 3 tier ₹ 300 72 berths per bogie Train has 8 bogies AC-3 tier ₹ 898 64 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-2 tier ₹ 1388 45 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-first class ₹ 2691 26 berths per bogie Train has 1 bogie The distance between P and D is 1100 km. Assume that the train does not stop at any station unless otherwise indicated. The running cost per kilometre: AC - bogie – ₹ 25, non-AC-bogie – ₹ 10
questions.question_text: What is the approximate profit for the railways if the X-expressway runs at full occupancy on a particular day?

questions.passage_text: Directions (Questions 92 to 96): Read the following information carefully and answer the questions that follow : A train journey from P to D by an X- express has 4 classes of fares 3 tier ₹ 300 72 berths per bogie Train has 8 bogies AC-3 tier ₹ 898 64 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-2 tier ₹ 1388 45 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-first class ₹ 2691 26 berths per bogie Train has 1 bogie The distance between P and D is 1100 km. Assume that the train does not stop at any station unless otherwise indicated. The running cost per kilometre: AC - bogie – ₹ 25, non-AC-bogie – ₹ 10
questions.question_text: Assuming full occupancy, a bogie of which class exhibits the highest profit margin?
questions.solution:
Clearly, we have:
Profit margin = Total fare collected - Total running cost
Profit margin exhibited by 3 tier
= ₹ [(8 $\times$ 72 $\times$ 300) - (8 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 1100)]
= ₹ (172800 - 88000) = ₹ 84800.
Profit margin exhibited by AC - 3 tier
= ₹ [(2 $\times$ 64 $\times$ 898) - (2 $\times$ 25 $\times$ 1100)] = ₹ (114944 - 55000)
= ₹ 59944.
Profit margin exhibited by AC - 2 tier
= ₹ [(2 $\times$ 45 $\times$ 1388) - (2 $\times$ 25 $\times$ 1100)]
= ₹ (124920 - 55000) = ₹ 69920.
Profit margin exhibited by AC-first class
= ₹ [(1 $\times$ 26 $\times$ 2691) - (1 $\times$ 25 $\times$ 1100)]
= ₹ (69966 - 27500) = ₹ 42466.
Clearly, it is the highest for 3 tier.

questions.passage_text: Directions (Questions 92 to 96): Read the following information carefully and answer the questions that follow : A train journey from P to D by an X- express has 4 classes of fares 3 tier ₹ 300 72 berths per bogie Train has 8 bogies AC-3 tier ₹ 898 64 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-2 tier ₹ 1388 45 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-first class ₹ 2691 26 berths per bogie Train has 1 bogie The distance between P and D is 1100 km. Assume that the train does not stop at any station unless otherwise indicated. The running cost per kilometre: AC - bogie – ₹ 25, non-AC-bogie – ₹ 10
questions.question_text: The highest revenue for a journey from P to D will always be generated by
questions.solution:
Revenue generated by 3 tier = ₹ (8 $\times$ 72 $\times$ 300) = ₹ 172800.
Revenue generated by AC-3 tier = ₹ (2 $\times$ 64 $\times$ 898)
= ₹ 114944.
Revenue generated by AC-2 tier = ₹ (2 $\times$ 45 $\times$ 1388)
= ₹ 124920.
Revenue generated by AC-first class = ₹ (1 $\times$ 26 $\times$ 2691)
= ₹ 69966.
Clearly, it is the highest for 3 tier.

questions.passage_text: Directions (Questions 92 to 96): Read the following information carefully and answer the questions that follow : A train journey from P to D by an X- express has 4 classes of fares 3 tier ₹ 300 72 berths per bogie Train has 8 bogies AC-3 tier ₹ 898 64 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-2 tier ₹ 1388 45 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-first class ₹ 2691 26 berths per bogie Train has 1 bogie The distance between P and D is 1100 km. Assume that the train does not stop at any station unless otherwise indicated. The running cost per kilometre: AC - bogie – ₹ 25, non-AC-bogie – ₹ 10
questions.question_text: Assuming full occupancy in all the classes, for a journey between P and D, the profit margin (as a percentage of running costs) of the class showing the lowest profit is approximately
questions.solution:
The profit margin collected by AC-first class is the lowest.
Required percentage = $\left(\frac{42466}{27500}\times100\right)\% = 154.4\%$.

questions.passage_text: Directions (Questions 92 to 96): Read the following information carefully and answer the questions that follow : A train journey from P to D by an X- express has 4 classes of fares 3 tier ₹ 300 72 berths per bogie Train has 8 bogies AC-3 tier ₹ 898 64 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-2 tier ₹ 1388 45 berths per bogie Train has 2 bogies AC-first class ₹ 2691 26 berths per bogie Train has 1 bogie The distance between P and D is 1100 km. Assume that the train does not stop at any station unless otherwise indicated. The running cost per kilometre: AC - bogie – ₹ 25, non-AC-bogie – ₹ 10
questions.question_text: For Q. 92, the percentage of the total profit that comes out of AC bogie is approximately
questions.solution:
As calculated in Q. 92,
Total profit = ₹ 257130.
Profit from AC bogies = ₹ [(114944 + 124920 + 69966)
- 137500] = ₹ (309830 - 137500) = ₹ 172330.
Required percentage = $\left(\frac{172330}{257130}\times100\right)\% = 67\% \approx 70\%$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper bought three varieties A, B and C of rice in different amounts at the rates of ₹ 34.50, ₹ 28.60 and ₹ 32.40 per kg respectively. In which of the following transactions will he gain maximum?
questions.option_a: He bought 25 kg of rice of variety C and sold at ₹ 42 per kg and he bought 30 kg of variety A and sold at ₹ 38 per kg.
questions.option_b: He bought 40 kg of rice of variety B and sold at ₹ 37 per kg and he bought 20 kg of variety A and sold at ₹ 40 per kg.
questions.option_c: He bought 20 kg of rice of varieties A and C each and sold at ₹ 38 and ₹ 36 per kg respectively.
questions.option_d: He bought 30 kg of rice of variety A and sold at ₹ 37 per kg and he bought 20 kg of rice of variety B and sold at ₹ 33 per kg.
questions.option_e: He bought 20 kg of rice of variety B and sold at ₹ 40 per kg and he bought 40 kg of rice of variety C and sold at ₹ 38 per kg.

questions.question_text: Nikita bought 30 kg of wheat at the rate of ₹ 9.50 per kg and 40 kg of wheat at the rate of ₹ 8.50 per kg and mixed them. She sold the mixture at the rate of ₹ 8.90 per kg. Her total profit or loss in the transaction was
Manish purchased 25 kg of rice @ ₹ 32 per kg and 15 kg of rice @ ₹ 36 per kg. He mixed the two varieties of rice and sold it @ ₹ 40.20 per kg. What is the percent profit earned?

One variety of sugar is sold for ₹ 3.20 per kg at a loss of 20% and another variety is sold for ₹ 6 per kg at a gain of 20%. If equal quantities of the two are mixed together and the mixture is sold at ₹ 5.40 per kg, what is the loss or gain percentage?
questions.solution:
C.P. per kg of first variety = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{80}\times3.20\right) = ₹ 4$.
C.P. per kg of second variety = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{120}\times6\right) = ₹ 5$.
C.P. of 2 kg of sugar = ₹ (4 + 5) = ₹ 9.
S.P. of 2 kg of sugar = ₹ (2 $\times$ 5.40) = ₹ 10.80.
Gain = ₹ (10.80 - 9) = ₹ 1.80.
Gain% = $\left(\frac{1.80}{9}\times100\right)\% = 20\%$.

questions.question_text: Arun purchased 30 kg of wheat at the rate of ₹ 11.50 per kg and 20 kg of wheat at the rate of ₹ 14.25 per kg. He mixed the two and sold the mixture. Approximately what price per kg should he sell the mixture to make 30% profit?

Padam purchased 30 kg of rice at the rate of ₹ 17.50 per kg and another 30 kg rice at a certain rate. He mixed the two and sold the entire quantity at the rate of ₹ 18.60 per kg and made 20% overall profit. At what price per kg did he purchase the lot of another 30 kg rice?
questions.solution:
Let the required price per kg be ₹ $x$. Then,
C.P. of 60 kg rice = ₹ $(30 	imes 17.50 + 30 	imes x) = ₹ (525 + 30x)$.
S.P. of 60 kg rice = ₹ $(60 	imes 18.60) = ₹ 1116$.
\ $\frac{1116 - (525 + 30x)}{525 + 30x} \times 100 = 20$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{591 - 30x}{525 + 30x} = \frac{1}{5}$

questions.question_text: A person blends two varieties of tea–one costing ₹ 160 per kg and the other costing ₹ 200 per kg in the ratio 5 : 4. He sells the blended variety at ₹ 192 per kg. His profit percent is
questions.solution:
Suppose he bought 5 kg and 4 kg of the two varieties
respectively.
C.P. of 9 kg = ₹ $(5 	imes 160 + 4 	imes 200) = ₹ 1600$.
S.P. of 9 kg = ₹ $(9 	imes 192) = ₹ 1728$.
\ Profit % = $\left(\frac{128}{1600} \times 100\right) \% = 8\%$.

questions.question_text: A trader mixes three varieties of groundnuts costing ₹ 50, ₹ 20 and ₹ 30 per kg in the ratio 2 : 4 : 3 in terms of weight, and sells the mixture at ₹ 33 per kg. What percentage of profit does he make?
questions.solution:
Suppose he bought 2 kg, 4 kg and 3 kg of the three
varieties respectively.
C.P. of 9 kg = ₹ $(2 	imes 50 + 4 	imes 20 + 3 	imes 30) = ₹ 270$.
S.P. of 9 kg = ₹ $(9 	imes 33) = ₹ 297$.
\ Profit% = $\left(\frac{27}{270} \times 100\right) \% = 10\%$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper bought 30 kg of wheat at the rate of ₹ 45 per kg. He sold forty percent of the total quantity at the rate of ₹ 50 per kg. Approximately at what price per kg should he sell the remaining quantity to make 25 percent overall profit?
questions.solution:
C.P. of 30 kg wheat = ₹ $(30 	imes 45) = ₹ 1350$.
Quantity of wheat sold = 40% of 30 kg = 12 kg.
Quantity left = $(30 - 12)$ kg = 18 kg.
For 25% profit, desired S.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{125}{100} \times 1350\right) = ₹ 1687.50$.
Money realised by selling 12 kg wheat = ₹ $(12 	imes 50) = ₹ 600$.
Balance required = ₹ $(1687.50 - 600) = ₹ 1087.50$.
\ Required price = ₹ $\left(\frac{1087.50}{18}\right)$ per kg
= ₹ 60.41 per kg $\approx$ ₹ 60 per kg.

questions.question_text: A dealer buys dry fruit at the rate of ₹ 100, ₹ 80 and ₹ 60 per kg. He bought them in the ratio 12 : 15 : 20 by weight. He in total gets 20% profit by selling the first two and at last he finds he has no gain no loss in selling the whole quantity which he had. What was the percentage loss he suffered for the third quantity?
questions.solution:
Suppose he bought 12 kg, 15 kg and 20 kg of the three
varieties respectively.
Then, total C.P. = ₹ $(12 	imes 100 + 15 	imes 80 + 20 	imes 60)$
= ₹ $(1200 + 1200 + 1200) = ₹ 3600$.
Let the loss on the third quantity be $x\%$.
Then, 120% of 2400 + $(100 - x)\%$ of 1200 = 3600
$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{6}{5} \times 2400\right) + \left(\frac{100 - x}{100} \times 1200\right) = 3600$
$\Rightarrow \frac{100 - x}{100} \times 1200 = 3600 - 2880 = 720$
$\Rightarrow 100 - x = \frac{720 \times 100}{1200} = 60$
$\Rightarrow x = 40\%$.

questions.question_text: A dairyman pays ₹ 6.40 per litre of milk. He adds water and sells the mixture at ₹ 8 per litre, thereby making 37.5% profit. The proportion of water to milk received by the customers is
questions.solution:
Mean cost price = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{137.5} \times 8\right) = ₹ \frac{64}{11}$.
By the rule of alligation :
C.P. of 1 litre water: 0
C.P. of 1 litre milk: 6.40
Mean price: $\frac{64}{11}$
\ Required ratio = $\left(6.40 - \frac{64}{11}\right) : \left(\frac{64}{11} - 0\right) = \frac{64}{110} : \frac{64}{11} = 1 : 10$.

questions.question_text: Five litres of water is added to a certain quantity of pure milk costing ₹ 3 per litre. If by selling the mixture at the same price as before, a profit of 20% is made, then what is the amount of pure milk in the mixture?
questions.solution:
Mean cost price = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{120} \times 3\right) = ₹ \frac{5}{2}$.
By the rule of alligation:
C.P. of 1 litre water: 0
C.P. of 1 litre milk: 3
Mean price: $\frac{5}{2}$
\ Ratio of water and milk = $\left(3 - \frac{5}{2}\right) : \left(\frac{5}{2} - 0\right) = \frac{1}{2} : \frac{5}{2} = 1 : 5$.
So, required quantity of milk = $(5 \times 5)$ litres = 25 litres.

questions.question_text: By mixing two brands of tea and selling the mixture at the rate of ₹ 177 per kg, a shopkeeper makes a profit of 18%. If to every 2 kg of one brand costing ₹ 200 per kg, 3 kg of the other brand is added, then how much per kg does the other brand cost ?
questions.solution:
Let the cost of the other brand be ₹ $x$ per kg.
C.P. of 5 kg = ₹ $(2 	imes 200 + 3 	imes x) = ₹ (400 + 3x)$.
S.P. of 5 kg = ₹ $(5 	imes 177) = ₹ 885$.
\ $\frac{885 - (400 + 3x)}{400 + 3x} \times 100 = 18$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{485 - 3x}{400 + 3x} = \frac{9}{50}$
$\Leftrightarrow 24250 - 150x = 3600 + 27x$
$\Leftrightarrow 177x = 20650 \Leftrightarrow x = \frac{350}{3} = 116\frac{2}{3}$.
So, cost of the other brand = ₹ 116.66.

questions.question_text: The manufacturer of a certain item can sell all he can produce at the selling price of ₹ 60 each. It costs him ₹ 40 in materials and labour to produce each item and he has overhead expenses of ₹ 3000 per week in order to operate the plant. The number of units he should produce and sell in order to make a profit of at least ₹ 1000 per week, is
questions.solution:
Suppose, he must produce $x$ items.
Then, C.P. = ₹ $(40x + 3000)$, S.P. = 60x.
\ $60x - (40x + 3000) = 1000$ or $20x = 4000$ or $x = 200$.

questions.question_text: A milkman cheats his customer in two ways. He mixes 10% water in pure milk and increases the price of milk by 10%. He purchases 20 kg pure milk at a rate of ₹ 15 per kg. His total profit by selling it is
questions.solution:
C.P. of 20 kg milk = ₹ $(20 \times 15) = ₹ 300$.
Quantity of water added = 10% of 20 kg = 2 kg.
S.P. of 1 kg mixture = 110% of ₹ $15 = ₹ 16.50$.
S.P. of 22 kg mixture = ₹ $(22 \times 16.50) = ₹ 363$.
\ Profit = ₹ $(363 - 300) = ₹ 63$.

questions.question_text: A dishonest dealer uses a scale of 90 cm instead of a metre scale and claims to sell at cost price. His profit is
questions.solution: Gain% = $\left(\frac{10}{90} \times 100\right) \% = 11\frac{1}{9}\%$.

questions.question_text: A dealer professes to sell his goods at cost price but he uses a false weight of 950 grams for a kilogram. The gain percent of the dealer is
questions.solution: Gain% = $\left(\frac{50}{950} \times 100\right) \% = 5\frac{5}{19}\%$.

questions.question_text: A fruit seller professes to sell his fruits at cost price, but still gains 25% on his outlay. What weight does he substitute for a kilogram?
questions.solution:
Let error = $x$ gms.
Then, $\frac{x}{1000 - x} \times 100 = 25$
$\Leftrightarrow 100x = 25000 - 25x \Leftrightarrow 125x = 25000$
$\Leftrightarrow x = 200$.
\ Weight used = $(1000 - 200)$ gms = 800 gms.

questions.question_text: A dishonest dealer professes to sell his goods at cost price. But he uses a false weight and thus gains $18\frac{6}{47}\%$. For a kg, he uses a weight of
questions.solution:
Let error = $x$ gms. Then,
$\frac{x}{1000 - x} \times 100 = \frac{300}{47}$
$\Leftrightarrow 47x = 3(1000 - x) \Leftrightarrow 50x = 3000 \Leftrightarrow x = 60$.
\ Weight used = $(1000 - 60) = 940$ gms.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper cheats to the extent of 10% while buying as well as selling, by using false weights. His total gain is

Left pan of a faulty balance weighs 100 grams more than its right pan. A shopkeeper keeps the weight measure in the left pan while buying goods but keeps it in the right pan while selling his goods. He uses only 1 kg weight measure. If he sells his goods at the listed cost price, what is his gain?
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. of 1 kg goods be ₹ 1.
Then, he buys 1100 g goods for ₹ 1 and sells 900 g goods
for ₹ 1.
\ C.P. of 1100 g goods = ₹ 1
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of 900 g goods = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{1100} \times 900\right) = ₹ \frac{9}{11}$.
S.P. of 900 g goods = ₹ 1.
Gain = ₹ $\left(1 - \frac{9}{11}\right) = ₹ \frac{2}{11}$.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{2}{11} \times \frac{11}{9} \times 100\right) \% = \frac{200}{9}\%$.

questions.question_text: A grocer sells rice at a profit of 10% and uses weights which are 20% less than the market weight. The total gain earned by him will be
questions.solution:
Let us consider a packet of rice marked 1 kg.
Its actual weight is 80% of 1000 gm = 800 gm.
Let C.P. of each gm be ₹ 1.
Then, C.P. of this packet = ₹ 800.
S.P. of this packet = 110% of
C.P. of 1 kg = ₹ $\left(\frac{110}{100} \times 1000\right) = ₹ 1100$.
\ Gain% = $\left(\frac{300}{800} \times 100\right) \% = 37.5\%$.
Short cut Method: Using the formula, we have:
Gain% = $\left\{\frac{10 + 20}{100 - 20} \times 100\right\} \%$
= $\left(\frac{30}{80} \times 100\right) \% = 37.5\%$.

questions.question_text: A dishonest dealer sells the goods at 20% loss on cost price but uses 15% less weight. What is his percentage profit or loss?
questions.solution:
Gain/Loss% = $\left\{\frac{-5 - 20}{100 - 15} \times 100\right\} \%$
= $\left(\frac{-5}{85} \times 100\right) \% = -\frac{100}{17}\%$
= $-5\frac{15}{17}\%$.
Since it is -ve, hence it is a loss.

questions.question_text: A merchant professes to lose 4% on a certain tea but he uses a weight equal to 840g instead of 1 kg. Find his real loss or gain percent.
questions.solution:
Percentage deduction in weight = $\left(\frac{160}{1000} \times 100\right) \% = 16\%$.
\ Gain/Loss% = $\left\{\frac{-16 - 4}{100 - 16} \times 100\right\} \%$
= $\left(\frac{12}{84} \times 100\right) \% = 14\frac{2}{7}\%$.
Since it is +ve, hence it is a gain.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper advertises for selling cloth at 4% loss. However, by using a false metre scale he actually gains 20%. What is the actual length of the scale?
questions.solution:
Let the percentage deduction in weight be $y \%$.
Then,
$\frac{y-4}{100-y} \times 100 = 20 \Rightarrow \frac{y-4}{100-y} = \frac{1}{5}$
$\Rightarrow 5y - 20 = 100 - y \Rightarrow 6y = 120 \Rightarrow y = 20$.
Hence, for a metre, length used = $(100 - 20)\%$ of $1$ m
$= 80\%$ of $100$ cm $= 80$ cm.

questions.question_text: Instead of a metre scale, a cloth merchant uses a 120 cm scale while buying but uses an 80 cm scale while selling the same cloth. If he offers a discount of 20% on cash payment what is his overall profit percentage?
questions.solution:
Let the actual C.P. of $1$ m cloth be ₹ $1$.
Then, the merchant buys $120$ cm cloth for ₹ $1$ and sells
$80$ cm cloth at a discount of $20\%$ on ₹ $1$.
So, C.P. of $120$ cm cloth = ₹ $1$
$\Rightarrow$ C.P. of $80$ cm cloth = ₹ $\left(\frac{1}{120} \times 80\right) = ₹ \frac{2}{3}$.
S.P. of $80$ cm cloth = $80\%$ of ₹ $1$ = ₹ $\frac{4}{5}$.
Profit = ₹ $\left(\frac{4}{5} - \frac{2}{3}\right) = ₹ \frac{2}{15}$.
$\therefore$ Profit $\%$ = $\left(\frac{2}{15} \times \frac{3}{2} \times 100\right) \% = 20\%$.

questions.question_text: A trader professes to sell his goods at a nominal gain percentage but actually earns $37\frac{1}{2}\%$ profit by using false weight. If for a kg he uses a weight of 800 gm, what is the nominal gain percentage at which he claims to be selling his goods?
questions.solution:
Let the required gain be $x\%$.
Percentage deduction in weight = $\left(\frac{200}{1000} \times 100\right) \% = 20\%$.
$\therefore \frac{20+x}{100-20} \times 100 = 37 \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{20+x}{80} = \frac{3}{8}$
$\Rightarrow 20 + x = 30 \Rightarrow x = 10$.
Hence, nominal gain percentage = $10\%$.

questions.question_text: A dry fruit merchant professes to sell 2 kg almond packs at a loss of 20%. However, he uses two false weights each of which is marked 1 kg and thus gains $6\frac{2}{3}\%$ on selling every 2 kg of almonds. If it is given that one of the weights weighs only 850 gm, then how much does the other weight weigh?
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. of $1$ kg of almonds be ₹ $100$.
Then, S.P. realised by the merchant on selling a $2$ kg pack
= ₹ $(2 \times 80) = ₹ 160$.
Profit = $6 \frac{2}{3} \%$.
C.P. of each pack
= ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{106 \frac{2}{3}} \times 160\right) = ₹ \left(100 \times \frac{3}{320} \times 160\right) = ₹ 150$.
Clearly, the $2$ kg pack has $1$ kg $500$ gm of almonds.
So, other weight = $(1$ kg $500$ gm $- 850$ gm$) = 650$ gm.

questions.question_text: A stockist wants to make some profit by selling sugar. He contemplates about various methods. Which of the following would maximize his profit?
questions.option_b: Use 900 g of weight instead of 1 kg
questions.option_c: Mix 10% impurities in sugar and sell sugar at cost price
questions.option_d: Increase the price by 5% and reduce the weight by 5%
questions.solution:
We find the net profit in each case :
(a) In this case, profit = $10\%$.
(b) Profit = $\left(\frac{100}{900} \times 100\right) \% = 11 \frac{1}{9} \%$.
(c) Let C.P. of sugar be ₹ $1$ per kg.
Then, he mixes $100$ gm impurities and realizes the C.P.
of $1.1$ kg sugar by selling $1$ kg of sugar. i.e., S.P. of sugar
= ₹ $1.10$ per kg.
$\therefore$ Profit = $\left(\frac{0.1}{1} \times 100\right) \% = 10\%$.
(d) Let C.P. of sugar be ₹ $1$ per kg.
Since he weighs $950$ gm instead of a kg, his actual
C.P. = ₹ $0.95$.
S.P. = $105\%$ of ₹ $1$ = ₹ $1.05$.
$\therefore$ Profit = $\left(\frac{0.10}{0.95} \times 100\right) \% = 10 \frac{10}{19} \%$.
Clearly, the maximum profit is earned when he used a
$900$ gm weight for a kg.

questions.question_text: A fair price shopkeeper takes 10% profit on his goods. He lost 20% goods during theft. His loss percent is
questions.solution:
Suppose he has $100$ items. Let C.P. of each item be ₹ $1$.
Total cost = ₹ $100$. Number of items left after theft = $80$.
S.P. of each item = ₹ $1.10$.
$\therefore$ Total sale = ₹ $(1.10 \times 80) = ₹ 88$.
Hence, Loss $\%$ = $\left(\frac{12}{100} \times 100\right) \% = 12\%$.

questions.question_text: Prateek sold a music system to Kartik at 20% gain and Kartik sold it to Swastik at 40% gain. If Swastik paid ₹ 10500 for the music system, what amount did Prateek pay for the same?
questions.solution:
Let the price paid by Prateek be ₹ $x$.
Then, $140\%$ of $120\%$ of $x = 10500$
$\Rightarrow \frac{140}{100} \times \frac{120}{100} \times x = 10500 \Rightarrow x = \left(10500 \times \frac{25}{42}\right) = 6250$.

questions.question_text: A manufacturer sells an article to a wholesale dealer at a profit of 20% and the wholesale dealer sells it to a retail merchant at a loss of 5%. Find the resultant loss or profit.
questions.solution:
Let the cost of the article be ₹ $x$.
Then, cost paid by retail merchant = $95\%$ of $120\%$ of ₹ $x$
= ₹ $\left(\frac{95}{100} \times \frac{120}{100} \times x\right) = ₹ \left(\frac{114}{100} x\right) = 114\%$ of ₹ $x$
$\therefore$ Gain = $14\%$.

questions.question_text: A bought a radio set and spent ₹ 110 on its repairs. He then sold it to B at 20% profit, B sold it to C at a loss of 10% and C sold it for ₹ 1188 at a profit of 10%. What is the amount for which A bought the radio set?
questions.solution:
$110\%$ of $90\%$ of $120\%$ of $A = 1188$
$\Rightarrow \frac{110}{100} \times \frac{90}{100} \times \frac{120}{100} A = 1188 \Rightarrow \frac{1188}{1000} A = 1188 \Rightarrow A = 1000$.
$\therefore$ A purchased it for ₹ $(1000 - 110) = ₹ 890$.

questions.question_text: A car worth ₹ 1,50,000 was sold by X to Y at 5% profit. Y sold the car back to X at 2% loss. In the entire transaction

A manufacturer sold a machine to a wholesale dealer at a profit of 10%. The wholesale dealer sold it to a retailer at a profit of 20%. While transporting some defect occurred in the machine and hence the retailer sold it at a loss of 5%. The customer paid ₹ 627. Find the cost of the machine for the manufacturer.
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. for the manufacturer be ₹ $x$.
Then, $95\%$ of $120\%$ of $110\%$ of $x = 627$
$\Rightarrow \frac{95}{100} \times \frac{120}{100} \times \frac{110}{100} \times x = 627 \Rightarrow x = 500$.

questions.question_text: Goods pass successively through the hands of three traders and each of them sells his goods at a profit of 25% of his cost price. If the last trader sold the goods for ₹ 250, then how much did the first trader pay for them?
questions.solution:
Let the cost paid by the first trader be ₹ $x$.
Then, $125\%$ of $125\%$ of $125\%$ of $x = 250$
$\Rightarrow \frac{125}{100} \times \frac{125}{100} \times \frac{125}{100} \times x = 250 \Rightarrow x = \left(250 \times \frac{64}{125}\right) = 128$.

questions.question_text: An article passing through two hands is sold at a profit of 38% at the original cost price. If the first dealer makes a profit of 20%, then the profit percent made by the second is
questions.solution:
Let the original cost of the article be ₹ $P$ and the profit
percent made by the second be $x\%$.
Then, $120\%$ of $(100 + x)\%$ of $P = 138\%$ of $P$
$\Rightarrow \frac{120}{100} \times \frac{(100 + x)}{100} = \frac{138}{100} \Rightarrow \frac{100 + x}{100} = \frac{23}{20}$
$\Rightarrow 100 + x = 115 \Rightarrow x = 15$.

questions.question_text: A merchant buys an article for ₹ 27 and sells it at a profit of 10 percent of the selling price. The selling price of the article is
questions.solution:
Let the S.P. be ₹ $x$. Then,
$x - 10\%$ of $x = 27 \Leftrightarrow 90\%$ of $x = 27 \Leftrightarrow x = \left(27 \times \frac{10}{9}\right) = 30$.

questions.question_text: By selling an article, a man makes a profit of 25% of its selling price. His profit percent is
questions.solution:
Let the S.P. be ₹ $x$. Profit = $25\%$
Then, C.P. = $x - 25\%$ of ₹ $x = 75\%$ of ₹ $x = ₹ \left(\frac{3}{4} x\right)$.
Profit = ₹ $\left(x - \frac{3}{4} x\right) = ₹ \frac{x}{4}$.
$\therefore$ Profit $\%$ = $\left(\frac{x}{4} \times \frac{4}{3x} \times 100\right) \% = 33 \frac{1}{3} \%$.

questions.question_text: If there is a profit of 20% on the cost price of an article, the percentage of profit calculated on its selling price will be
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$. Then, profit = $20\%$ of ₹ $x = ₹ \frac{x}{5}$.
S.P. = ₹ $\left(x + \frac{x}{5}\right) = ₹ \frac{6x}{5}$.
$\therefore$ Desired profit $\%$ = $\left(\frac{x}{5} \times \frac{5}{6x} \times 100\right) \% = 16 \frac{2}{3} \%$.

questions.question_text: Vineet calculates his profit percentage on the selling price whereas Roshan calculates his profit on the cost price. They find that the difference of their profits is ₹ 275. If the selling price of both of them are the same and Vineet gets 25% profit whereas Roshan gets 15% profit, find their selling price.
questions.solution:
Let Roshan's C.P. = ₹ $p$.
Profit = $15\%$ of ₹ $p = ₹ \frac{3}{20} p$.
S.P. = ₹ $\left(p + \frac{3}{20} p\right) = ₹ \frac{23}{20} p$.
Profit $\%$ on S.P. = $\left(\frac{3}{20} p \times \frac{20}{23p} \times 100\right) \% = \left(\frac{300}{23}\right) \%$.
Now, let S.P. of each be ₹ $x$.
Then, $25\%$ of $x - \frac{300}{23}\%$ of $275 = 0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{275}{23} x = 27500 \Rightarrow x = \left(\frac{27500 \times 23}{275}\right) = 2300$.

questions.question_text: A clock was sold for ₹ 144. If the percentage of profit was numerically equal to the cost price, the cost of the clock was
questions.solution:
Let C.P. = ₹ $x$, Profit $\% = x\%$ and S.P. = ₹ $144$.
$\therefore x = \left[\frac{100}{(100 + x)} \times 144\right] \Rightarrow x(100 + x) = 14400$
$\Rightarrow x^2 + 100x - 14400 = 0$
$\Rightarrow x^2 + 180x - 80x - 14400 = 0$
$\Rightarrow (x + 180)(x - 80) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 80$.

questions.question_text: By selling an article at $\frac{2}{3}$ of the marked price, there is a loss of 10%. The profit percent, when the article is sold at the marked price, is
questions.solution:
Let the M.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, S.P. = ₹ $\frac{2}{3} x$, Loss = $10\%$.
C.P. = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{90} \times \frac{2}{3} x\right) = ₹ \frac{20}{27} x$.
If an article is sold at M.P., then
Profit = ₹ $\left(x - \frac{20}{27} x\right) = ₹ \frac{7x}{27}$.
$\therefore$ Profit $\%$ = $\left(\frac{7x}{27} \times \frac{27}{20x} \times 100\right) \% = 35\%$.

questions.question_text: Raghavan purchase a scooter at $\frac{13}{15}$ of its selling price and sold it at 12% more than its selling price. His gain is
questions.solution:
Let S.P. be ₹ $x$. Then, C.P.
= ₹ $\frac{13}{15} x$, Receipt = $112\%$ of ₹ $x = ₹ \frac{28}{25} x$.
Gain = ₹ $\left(\frac{28}{25} x - \frac{13}{15} x\right) = ₹ \frac{19x}{75}$.
$\therefore$ Gain $\%$ = $\left(\frac{19x}{75} \times \frac{15}{13x} \times 100\right) \% = \left(\frac{380}{13}\right) \% = 29 \frac{3}{13} \%$.

questions.question_text: A man buys an article for 10% less than its value and sells it for 10% more than its value. His gain or loss percent is
questions.solution:
Let the article be worth ₹ $x$.
C.P. = 90% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $ rac{9}{10}x$
S.P. = 110% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $ rac{11}{10}x$
Gain = ₹ $ rac{11}{10}x -  rac{9}{10}x =  rac{x}{5}$
Gain% = $ rac{x}{5} 	imes  rac{10}{9x} 	imes 100$ % = $22 rac{2}{9}$% > 20%.

questions.question_text: Samant bought a microwave oven and paid 10% less than the original price. He sold it with 30% profit on the price he had paid. What percentage of profit did Samant earn on the original price?
questions.solution:
Let original price = ₹ 100.
Then, C.P. = ₹ 90, S.P. = 130% of ₹ 90 = ₹ $ rac{130}{100} 	imes 90$ = ₹ 117.
Required percentage = (117 - 100)% = 17%.

questions.question_text: If 5% more is gained by selling an article for ₹ 350 than by selling it for ₹ 340, the cost of the article is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, 5% of $x$ = (350 - 340) = 10
$ rac{5}{100}x = 10 ightarrow x = 200$.

questions.question_text: If a man reduces the selling price of a fan from ₹ 400 to ₹ 380, his loss increases by 2%. The cost price of the fan is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$. Then, 2% of $x$ = (400 - 380) = 20
$ rac{2}{100}x = 20 ightarrow x = 1000$.

questions.question_text: An increase of ₹ 3 in the selling price of an article turns a loss of $17\frac{1}{2}\%$ into a gain of $17\frac{1}{2}\%$. The cost price (in ₹) of the article is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$.
Then, $107 rac{1}{2}$% of $x - 92 rac{1}{2}$% of $x$ = 3
$ rac{215}{200}x -  rac{185}{200}x = 3 ightarrow  rac{30}{200}x = 3 ightarrow x = 20$.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper sells an article at $12\frac{1}{2}\%$ loss. If he sells it for ₹ 92.50 more then he gains 6%. What is the cost price of the article?
A dealer sold an article at a loss of $2\frac{1}{2}\%$. Had he sold it for ₹ 100 more, he would have gained $7\frac{1}{2}\%$. To gain $12\frac{1}{2}\%$, he should sell it for
A man sells a book at a profit of 20%. If he had bought it at 20% less and sold it for ₹ 18 less, he would have gained 25%. The cost price of the book is
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. be ₹ 100. Then, profit = 20%. S.P. = ₹ 120.
New C.P. = 20% less = ₹ 80.
If gain is 25% then S.P. = ₹ $ rac{125}{100} 	imes 80 = 100$.
Difference in S.P. = ₹ (120 - 100) = ₹ 20.
If difference in S.P. is ₹ 20, then C.P. = ₹ 100.
If difference in S.P. is ₹ 18, then C.P. = ₹ $ rac{100}{20} 	imes 18 = 90$.

questions.question_text: A bookseller sells a book at a profit of 10%. If he had bought it at 4% less and sold it for ₹ 6 more, he would have gained $18\frac{3}{4}\%$. The cost price of the book is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of the book be ₹ 100.
Profit = 10%. S.P. = ₹ 110.
New C.P. = 4% less = ₹ 96.
If gain is $18 rac{3}{4}$%, then S.P. = $118 rac{3}{4}$% of 96
= ₹ $ rac{475}{4 	imes 100} 	imes 96 = 114$.
Difference in S.P. = ₹ (114 - 110) = ₹ 4.
If difference in S.P. is ₹ 4, C.P. = ₹ 100.
If difference in S.P. is ₹ 6, C.P. = ₹ $ rac{100}{4} 	imes 6 = 150$.

questions.question_text: A watch is sold at a profit of 20%. If both the cost price and the selling price of the watch are decreased by ₹ 100, the profit would be 5% more. Original cost price of the watch is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. be ₹ $x$. Profit = 20%. S.P. = 120% of ₹ $x$ = ₹ $ rac{6}{5}x$.
New C.P. = ₹ ($x$ - 100). New S.P. = ₹ ($ rac{6}{5}x$ - 100).
Profit = ₹ [($ rac{6}{5}x$ - 100) - ($x$ - 100)] = ₹ $ rac{x}{5}$.
$ rac{x}{5} 	imes  rac{1}{x - 100} 	imes 100 = 25 ightarrow 20(x - 100) = 5(x - 100)$ (error in solution text, calculation follows: $ rac{x}{5(x-100)} 	imes 100 = 25 ightarrow 20x = 25(x-100) ightarrow 5x = 2500 ightarrow x = 500$).

questions.question_text: An article is sold at a profit of 20%. If the cost price is increased by 10% and the sale price by ₹ 26, then the percentage of profit reduces by 5%. Determine the cost price.
questions.solution:
Let the C.P. be ₹ $x$. Profit = 20%.
S.P. = ₹ $ rac{120}{100} 	imes x =  rac{6x}{5}$.
New C.P. = ₹ $ rac{110}{100} 	imes x =  rac{11x}{10}$.
New S.P. = ₹ ($ rac{6x}{5} + 26$).
New profit = ₹ [($ rac{6x}{5} + 26$) - $ rac{11x}{10}$] = ₹ ($ rac{x}{10} + 26$).
($ rac{x}{10} + 26$) $	imes  rac{10}{11x} 	imes 100 = 15 ightarrow 100(x + 260) = 165x ightarrow 65x = 26000 ightarrow x = 400$.

questions.question_text: The difference between the cost price and sale price of an article is ₹ 240. If the profit is 20%, the selling price is
The cash difference between the selling prices of an article at a profit of 4% and 6% is ₹ 3. The ratio of the two selling prices is
questions.solution:
Let C.P. of the article be ₹ $x$.
Then, Required ratio = $ rac{104% 	ext{ of } x}{106% 	ext{ of } x} =  rac{104}{106} =  rac{52}{53} = 52 : 53$.

questions.question_text: Shailja earns 15 percent on an investment but loses 10 percent on another investment. If the ratio of the two investments is 3 : 5, then the combined loss percent is
questions.solution:
Let the two investments be ₹ $3x$ and ₹ $5x$ respectively.
Then, total investment = ₹ ($3x + 5x$) = ₹ $8x$.
Total output = ₹ (115% of $3x$ + 90% of $5x$)
= ₹ ($3.45x + 4.5x$) = ₹ $7.95x$.
Loss = ₹ ($8x - 7.95x$) = ₹ $0.05x$.
Loss percent = $ rac{0.05x}{8x} 	imes 100$ % = $ rac{5}{8}$%.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper bought three watches $w_1$, $w_2$ and $w_3$ from a dealer and sold them to three different customers. The ratio of the selling prices of the watches $w_1$, $w_2$ and $w_3$ was 2 : 3 : 4. The shopkeeper gains 30% and 20% on the watches $w_1$ and $w_2$ respectively but loses 40% on the watch $w_3$. What was the shopkeeper’s approximate percent gain or loss in the whole transaction?
questions.solution:
Let the S.P. of watches $w_1, w_2$ and $w_3$ be ₹ $2x$, ₹ $3x$ and ₹ $4x$ respectively.
Gain on watch $w_1$ = 30%.
C.P. of watch $w_1$ = ₹ $ rac{100}{130} 	imes 2x =  rac{20x}{13}$.
Gain on watch $w_2$ = 20%.
C.P. of watch $w_2$ = ₹ $ rac{100}{120} 	imes 3x =  rac{5x}{2}$.
Loss on watch $w_3$ = 40%.
C.P. of watch $w_3$ = ₹ $ rac{100}{60} 	imes 4x =  rac{20x}{3}$.
Total C.P. of three watches = ₹ ($ rac{20x}{13} +  rac{5x}{2} +  rac{20x}{3}$) = ₹ $ rac{835x}{78}$.
Total S.P. of three watches = ₹ ($2x + 3x + 4x$) = ₹ $9x$.
Loss = ₹ ($ rac{835x}{78} - 9x$) = ₹ $ rac{133x}{78}$.
Loss% = $ rac{133x}{78} 	imes  rac{78}{835x} 	imes 100$ % = $ rac{2660}{167}$% $ightarrow$ 15.93% $ightarrow$ 16%.

questions.question_text: A man sells two articles for ₹ 240 each. On one he gains 20% and on the other he loses 20%. What is the gain or loss percent in the entire transaction?
questions.solution: Loss% = $ rac{(	ext{Common Gain & Loss%})^2}{10} = ( rac{20}{10})^2$ % = 4%.

questions.question_text: A shopkeeper sells two watches for ₹ 308 each. On one he gets 12% profit and on the other 12% loss. His profit or loss in the entire transaction was
A man sells two flats at the rate of ₹ 1.995 lakhs each. On one he gains 5% and on the other, he loses 5%. His gain or loss percent in the whole transaction is
